576	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
577	\label{sec:gr_glued}
578	
579	\begin{definition}
580	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
581	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
582	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
583	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
584	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
585	  % affine patches" (read from
586	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
587	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
588	  % "Therefore the schemes
589	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
590	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
591	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
592	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
593	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
594	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
595	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
596	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
597	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
598	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
599	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
600	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
601	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
602	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
603	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
604	  \[
605	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
606	  \]
607	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
608	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
609	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
610	
611	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
612	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
613	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
614	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
615	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
616	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
617	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
618	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
619	  follows.
620	\end{definition}
621	
622	\section{Separatedness}
623	\label{sec:gr_separated}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
627	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
628	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
629	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
630	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
631	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
633	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
634	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
635	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
636	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
637	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
638	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
639	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
640	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
641	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
645	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
646	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
647	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
648	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
649	\begin{proof}
650	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
651	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
652	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
653	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
654	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
655	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
656	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
657	  homomorphism is surjective.
658	
659	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
660	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
661	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
662	  \[
663	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
664	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
665	    \qquad
666	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
667	  \]
668	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
669	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
670	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
671	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
672	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
673	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
674	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
675	  and in particular
676	  \[
677	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
678	  \]
679	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
680	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
681	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
682	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
683	  \[
684	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
685	  \]
686	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
687	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
688	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
689	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
690	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
691	\end{proof}

693	\section{Properness}
694	\label{sec:gr_proper}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
698	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
699	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
700	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
701	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from

712	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
713	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
714	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
715	  %
716	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
717	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
718	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
719	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
720	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
721	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
722	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
723	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
724	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
725	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
726	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
727	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
728	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
729	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
730	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
731	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
732	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
733	\end{lemma}
734	
735	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
736	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
737	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
738	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
739	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
740	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
741	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
742	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
743	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
744	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
745	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
746	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
747	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
748	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
749	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
750	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
751	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
752	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
753	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
754	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
755	\begin{proof}
756	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
757	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
758	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
759	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
760	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
761	  \[
762	    \begin{array}{ccc}
763	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
764	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
765	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
766	    \end{array}
767	  \]
768	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
769	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
770	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
771	
772	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
773	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
774	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
775	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
776	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
777	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
778	  \(K\). Define
779	  \[
780	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
781	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
782	  \]
783	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
784	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
785	  landing in \(U^J\).
786	
787	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
788	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
789	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
790	  \[
791	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
792	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
793	    \qquad
794	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
795	  \]
796	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
797	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
798	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
799	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
800	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
801	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
802	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
803	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
804	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
805	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
806	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
807	
808	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
809	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
810	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
811	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
812	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
813	  proper.
814	\end{proof}

816	\section{Out of scope}
817	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
818	
819	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
820	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
821	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
822	are not treated here:
823	\begin{itemize}
824	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
825	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
826	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
827	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
828	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
829	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
830	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
831	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
832	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
833	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
834	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
835	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
836	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
837	    that base change.
838	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function

82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE (iter-007): blocked on absent tensor/monoidal structure for SheafOfModules.
160	  % The degree-m component is Gamma(X_s, L_s^{otimes m}); Mathlib at the pinned commit has no
161	  % MonoidalCategoryStruct on X.Modules (synthesis fails) and no SheafOfModules/PresheafOfModules
162	  % tensor lemmas, so L^{otimes m} cannot even be named. This is a deep infrastructure prerequisite
163	  % (tensor product of sheaves of modules + lax-monoidal global sections), to be blueprinted as a
164	  % separate multi-lemma sub-build. See task_results .../QuotScheme.md.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}
199	
200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
308	  (rank--nullity)]
309	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
310	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
311	  \mathlibok
312	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
313	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
314	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
315	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
316	  \[
317	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
318	  \]
319	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
320	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
321	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
322	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
323	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
324	\end{lemma}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
327	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
328	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
329	  \mathlibok
330	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
331	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
332	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
333	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
334	  inverse
335	  \[
336	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
337	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
338	  \]
339	  Together with the coercion
340	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
341	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
342	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
343	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
344	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
345	\end{lemma}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
351	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
352	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
353	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
355	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
356	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
357	  %  function
358	  %  $$
359	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
360	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
361	  %  $$
362	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
363	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
364	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
365	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Encode the graded ring as a graded
366	  %   `κ`-algebra `𝒜 : ℕ → Submodule κ A` with `[GradedAlgebra 𝒜]`, degree-0 part
367	  %   the scalars (`𝒜 0 = (1 : Submodule κ A)`, so `S₀ = κ` is a field), and the
368	  %   module as `ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M` with `[SetLike.GradedSMul 𝒜 ℳ]`
369	  %   (`[GradedModule 𝒜 ℳ]`). "Generated in degree 1" is
370	  %   `Algebra.adjoin κ (𝒜 1 : Set A) = ⊤`. The Hilbert function is
371	  %   `n ↦ Module.finrank κ (ℳ n)`. The conclusion is stated in the
372	  %   rational-series form CONSUMED by `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly`
373	  %   (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib), i.e. via `PowerSeries.invOneSubPow`:
374	  %     {κ : Type*} [Field κ] {A : Type*} [CommRing A] [Algebra κ A]
375	  %       [IsNoetherianRing A] (𝒜 : ℕ → Submodule κ A) [GradedAlgebra 𝒜]
376	  %       (hgen : Algebra.adjoin κ (↑(𝒜 1) : Set A) = ⊤)
377	  %       {M : Type*} [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M]
378	  %       (ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M) [GradedModule 𝒜 ℳ]
379	  %       [∀ n, FiniteDimensional κ (ℳ n)] :
380	  %       ∃ (p : Polynomial ℚ) (d : ℕ) (N : ℕ), ∀ n > N,
381	  %         (Module.finrank κ (ℳ n) : ℚ)
382	  %           = (↑(Polynomial.toPowerSeries p) * ↑(PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ d)
383	  %               : ℚ⟦X⟧).coeff n
384	  %   The exact graded-encoding typeclasses (`DirectSum.Decomposition`, the
385	  %   `S₀ = κ` identification, the `Polynomial.toPowerSeries`/unit coercions) are
386	  %   pinned by the scaffold pass; the load-bearing data are: finite-dimensionality
387	  %   of each `ℳ n` over `κ`, finite generation of `M`, degree-1 generation, and
388	  %   the `existsUnique_hilbertPoly`-compatible conclusion shape.
389	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
390	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
391	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
392	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
393	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
394	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
395	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
396	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
397	  such that for all \(n > N\)
398	  \[
399	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
400	  \]
401	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
402	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
403	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
404	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
405	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
406	
407	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
408	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
409	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
410	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
411	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
412	  rational series from the module.
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
416	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
417	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
418	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
419	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
420	%  following short exact sequence
421	%  $$
422	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
423	%  $$
424	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
425	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
426	\begin{proof}
427	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
428	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
429	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
430	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
431	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
432	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
433	
434	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
435	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
436	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
437	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
438	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
439	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
440	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
441	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
442	
443	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
444	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
445	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
446	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
447	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
448	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
449	
450	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
451	  degree-one generators, so that the induction hypothesis applies to finitely
452	  generated graded modules over \(R/(x)\), itself a graded quotient of
453	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\). Multiplication by \(x\) raises degree by one
454	  and assembles, for every \(n\), into a short exact sequence of
455	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector spaces
456	  \[
457	    0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{\;x\;} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0,
458	  \]
459	  where \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M / xM\) are finitely
460	  generated graded \(R\)-modules annihilated by \(x\), hence finitely generated
461	  graded modules over \(R/(x)\). By additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact
462	  sequence (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}, applied to the kernel and
463	  cokernel of the map \(x\)),
464	  \[
465	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
466	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
467	  \]
468	  for every \(n\). Multiplying the Hilbert series of \(M\) by \((1 - X)\) and
469	  summing this identity against \(X^{n}\) shows that
470	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) equals the Hilbert series of
471	  \(C\) minus that of \(K\), up to a polynomial in low degrees. By the induction
472	  hypothesis applied to the \(R/(x)\)-modules \(K\) and \(C\) (which live over
473	  \(r - 1\) variables), each of those Hilbert series is, up to a polynomial, of
474	  the form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\) with \(d - 1 \le r - 1\). Hence
475	  \[
476	    (1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
477	      \;=\; q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
478	  \]
479	  and multiplying by the inverse power series
480	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1) = (1 - X)^{-1}\)
481	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) gives
482	  \[
483	    \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
484	      \;=\; p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
485	  \]
486	  with \(d \le r\). Reading off the coefficient of \(X^n\) for \(n \gg 0\) yields
487	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
488	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\),
489	  which is the claim.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
493	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
494	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
495	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
496	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
497	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
498	  % Polynomials" (read from
499	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
500	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
501	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
502	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
503	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
504	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
505	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
506	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
507	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
508	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
509	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
510	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
511	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
512	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
513	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
514	  \[
515	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
516	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
517	  \]
518	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
519	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
520	\end{theorem}
521	
522	\begin{proof}
523	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
524	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
525	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
526	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
527	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
528	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
529	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
530	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
531	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
532	  function
533	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
534	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
535	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
536	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
537	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
538	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
539	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
540	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
541	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
542	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
543	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
544	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
545	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
546	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
547	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
548	\end{proof}
549	
550	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
551	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
552	
553	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
554	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
555	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
556	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
557	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
558	
559	\begin{definition}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
563	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
565	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
566	  % NOTE (iter-011): the DEFINITION itself is now formalized and axiom-clean (via the
567	  % always-available `ofIdeals` construction; the `ofIdeals` inclusion direction is the
568	  % landed lemma `Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le`). Only the FORWARD characterization
569	  % (the reverse inclusion identifying the annihilator with the module-annihilator on
570	  % affine opens) remains bridge-gated on lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen
571	  % (section restriction as a localization for quasi-coherent sheaves on a general scheme),
572	  % which is not yet formally closed.
573	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
574	  % Polynomials" (read from
575	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
576	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
577	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
578	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
579	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
580	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
581	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
582	  project-built primitive.}
583	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
584	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
585	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
586	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
587	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
588	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
589	  \(U \subseteq X\),
590	  \[
591	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
592	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
593	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
594	  \]
595	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
596	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
597	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
598	
599	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
600	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
601	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
602	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
603	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
604	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
605	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
606	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
607	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
608	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
609	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
610	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
611	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
612	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
613	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
614	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
615	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
616	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
617	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
618	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
619	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
620	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
621	
622	  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
623	  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
624	\end{definition}
625	
626	\begin{lemma}\leanok
627	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
628	  localization]
629	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
630	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
631	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
632	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
633	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
634	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
635	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
636	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
637	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
638	  \[
639	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
640	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
641	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
642	  \]
643	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
644	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
645	
646	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
647	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
648	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
649	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
650	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
651	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
652	\end{lemma}
653	
654	\begin{proof}
655	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
656	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
657	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
658	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
659	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
660	
661	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
662	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
663	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
664	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
665	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
666	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
667	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
668	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
669	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
670	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
671	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
672	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
673	\end{proof}
674	
675	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
676	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
677	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
678	  \mathlibok
679	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
680	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
681	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
682	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
683	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
684	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
685	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
686	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
687	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
688	\end{lemma}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
691	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
692	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
693	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
694	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
695	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
696	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
697	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
698	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
699	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
700	  %  associated to $M$.
701	  %  [...]
702	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
703	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
704	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). For a quasi-coherent sheaf of
705	  %   modules `F : X.Modules` (`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent F`), an affine open
706	  %   `U : X.Opens` (`IsAffineOpen U`), and `f : Γ(X, U)` (the ring of sections,
707	  %   `X.presheaf.obj (op U)`), the section-restriction along the basic-open
708	  %   inclusion `D(f) = X.basicOpen f ⊆ U` exhibits `Γ(F, D(f))` as the
709	  %   localization of the `Γ(X, U)`-module `Γ(F, U)` at `Submonoid.powers f`.
710	  %   Write `ρ : Γ(F, U) →ₗ[Γ(X, U)] Γ(F, D(f))` for that restriction map (the
711	  %   additive `F.presheaf.map (homOfLE hD).op` made `Γ(X,U)`-linear by restriction
712	  %   of scalars along the ring map `Γ(X, U) → Γ(X, D(f))`). Then:
713	  %     {X : Scheme.{u}} (F : X.Modules) [SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent F]
714	  %       {U : X.Opens} (hU : IsAffineOpen U) (f : Γ(X, U)) :
715	  %       IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) ρ
716	  %   The companion ring statement `Γ(X, D(f)) = (Γ(X, U))_f`
717	  %   (`IsLocalization.Away f (Γ(X, D(f)))`) is lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib;
718	  %   the scalar tower `Γ(X, U) → Γ(X, D(f)) → Γ(F, D(f))` and the exact
719	  %   `Hom`/`presheaf.map` spelling of `ρ` are pinned by the scaffold pass.
720	  %   NOTE: only quasi-coherence is needed for this localization bridge — the
721	  %   finite-type hypothesis enters later (in def:modules_annihilator, via
722	  %   lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map), not here.
723	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
724	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
725	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
726	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
727	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
728	  \begin{enumerate}
729	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
730	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
731	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
732	    \item the section-restriction map
733	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
734	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
735	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
736	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
737	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
738	  \end{enumerate}
739	\end{lemma}
740	
741	\begin{proof}
742	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
743	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
744	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
745	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
746	
747	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
748	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
749	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
750	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
751	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
752	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
753	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
754	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
755	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
756	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
757	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
758	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
759	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
760	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
761	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
762	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
763	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
764	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
765	\end{proof}

767	\begin{definition}
768	
769	\leanok
770	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
771	  \label{def:schematic_support}
772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
773	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
774	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
775	  % Polynomials" (read from
776	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
777	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
778	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
779	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
780	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
781	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
782	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
783	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
784	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
785	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
786	  \[
787	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
788	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
789	  \]
790	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
791	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
792	  \(X\).
793	\end{definition}
794	
795	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
796	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
797	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
798	  \mathlibok
799	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
800	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
801	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
802	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
803	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
804	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
805	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
806	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
807	\end{lemma}
808	
809	\begin{definition}
810	
811	\leanok
812	[Proper support over the base]
813	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
814	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
815	  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
816	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
817	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
818	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
819	  % + L418-L419).
820	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
821	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
822	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
823	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
824	  %  [...]
825	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
826	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
827	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
828	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
829	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
830	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
831	  \[
832	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
833	  \]
834	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
835	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
836	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
837	  functor's pullback action requires.
838	\end{definition}

840	\begin{definition}
841	
842	\leanok
843	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
844	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}

847	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from

849	  % + L584-L585).

857	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
858	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
859	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
860	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for

867	\label{sec:quot_functor}
868

910	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
911	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
912	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the

916	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
917	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
918	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
919	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
920	  \[
921	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
922	  \]
923	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
924	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
925	  \begin{enumerate}
926	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
927	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
928	      which is flat over \(T\);
929	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
930	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
931	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
932	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
933	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
934	      \(\Phi\).
935	  \end{enumerate}
936	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
937	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
938	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
939	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
940	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
941	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
942	\end{definition}

944	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
945	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
946	
947	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
948	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
949	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
950	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
