1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	
97	\leanok
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  \leanok
127	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
128	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
129	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
130	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
131	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
132	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
133	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
134	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
135	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
136	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
137	\end{proof}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}\leanok
140	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
141	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
143	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
144	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
145	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
146	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
147	\end{lemma}
148	
149	\begin{proof}
150	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
151	  \leanok
152	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
153	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
154	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
155	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
156	  affine opens.
157	\end{proof}
158	
159	\subsection{The affine computation}
160	
161	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
162	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
163	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
164	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
165	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
166	
167	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
168	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
169	their proof sketches.
170	
171	\begin{lemma}
172	
173	\leanok
174	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
175	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
177	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
179	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
180	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
181	  \[
182	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
183	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
184	  \]
185	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
186	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
187	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
188	\end{lemma}
189	
190	\begin{proof}
191	
192	
193	
194	\leanok
195	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
196	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
197	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
198	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
199	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
200	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
201	\end{proof}
202	
203	\begin{lemma}
204	
205	\leanok
206	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
207	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
208	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
209	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
210	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
211	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
212	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
213	  \[
214	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
215	    \;\cong\;
216	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
217	  \]
218	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
219	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
220	  of \(N\).
221	\end{lemma}
222	
223	\begin{proof}
224	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
225	  \leanok
226	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
227	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
228	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
229	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
230	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
231	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
232	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
233	  equation of
234	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
235	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}
239	
240	\leanok
241	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
242	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
244	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
245	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
246	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
247	  \[
248	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
249	    \;\cong\;
250	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
251	  \]
252	\end{lemma}
253	
254	\begin{proof}
255	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
256	  \leanok
257	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
258	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
259	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
260	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
261	\end{proof}
262	
263	\begin{lemma}
264	
265	\leanok
266	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
267	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
269	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
270	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
271	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
272	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
273	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
274	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
275	  \[
276	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
277	    \;\cong\;
278	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
279	  \]
280	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
281	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
282	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
283	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
284	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
285	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
286	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
287	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
288	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
289	\end{lemma}
290	
291	\begin{proof}
292	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
293	  \leanok
294	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
295	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
296	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
297	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
298	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
299	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
300	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
301	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
302	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
303	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
304	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
305	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
306	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
307	
308	  \medskip
309	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
310	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
311	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
313	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
314	  \(U \mapsto
315	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
316	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
317	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
318	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
319	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
320	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
321	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
322	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
323	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
324	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
325	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
326	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
327	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
329	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
330	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
331	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
332	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
333	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
334	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
335	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
336	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
337	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
338	\end{proof}
339	
340	\begin{lemma}
341	
342	\leanok
343	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
344	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
346	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
347	  restriction map
348	  \[
349	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
350	  \]
351	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
352	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
353	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
354	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
355	\end{lemma}
356	
357	\begin{proof}
358	
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
362	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
363	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
364	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
365	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
366	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
367	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
368	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
369	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
370	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
374	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
375	on their proof sketches.
376	
377	\begin{lemma}
378	
379	\leanok
380	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
381	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
382	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
383	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
384	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
385	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
386	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
387	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
388	  itself.
389	\end{lemma}
390	
391	\begin{proof}
392	
393	
394	
395	\leanok
396	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
397	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
398	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
399	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
400	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
401	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
402	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
403	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
404	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
405	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
406	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
407	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
408	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
409	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
410	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
411	  basic-open computation below.
412	\end{proof}
413	
414	\begin{lemma}
415	
416	\leanok
417	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
418	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
419	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
420	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
421	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
422	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
423	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
424	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
425	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
426	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
427	\end{lemma}
428	
429	\begin{proof}
430	
431	
432	
433	\leanok
434	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
435	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
436	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
437	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
438	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
439	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
440	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
441	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
442	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
443	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
444	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
445	\end{proof}
446	
447	\begin{lemma}
448	
449	\leanok
450	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
451	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
453	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
454	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
455	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
456	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
457	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
458	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
459	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
460	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
461	  \[
462	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
463	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
464	  \]
465	\end{lemma}
466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
469	  \leanok
470	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
471	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
472	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
473	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
474	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
475	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
476	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
477	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
478	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
479	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
480	\end{proof}
481	
482	\begin{lemma}\leanok
483	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
484	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
485	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
486	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
487	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
488	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
489	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
490	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
491	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
492	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
493	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
494	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
495	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
496	% is hand-proved per a.
497	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
498	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
499	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
500	  \[
501	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
502	    \;\cong\;
503	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
504	  \]
505	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
506	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
507	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
508	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
509	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
510	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
511	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
512	
513	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
514	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
515	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
516	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
517	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
518	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
519	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
520	\end{lemma}
521	
522	\begin{proof}
523	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
524	  \leanok
525	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
526	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
527	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
528	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
529	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
530	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
531	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
532	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
533	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
534	
535	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
536	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
537	  \[
538	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
539	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
540	  \]
541	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
542	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
543	
544	  \medskip
545	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
546	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
547	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
548	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
549	
550	  \medskip
551	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
552	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
553	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
554	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
555	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
556	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
557	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
558	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
559	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
560	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
561	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
562	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
563	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
564	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
565	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
566	  an isomorphism.
567	
568	  \medskip
569	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
570	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
571	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
572	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
573	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
574	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
575	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
576	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
577	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
578	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
579	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
580	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
581	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
582	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
583	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
584	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
585	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
586	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
587	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
588	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
589	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
591	  movements.
592	
593	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
594	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
595	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
596	  \[
597	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
598	      \;\cong\;
599	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
600	  \]
601	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
602	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
603	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
604	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
605	
606	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
607	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
608	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
609	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
610	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
611	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
612	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
613	  \[
614	    \begin{array}{ccc}
615	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
616	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
617	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
618	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
619	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
620	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
621	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
622	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
623	    \end{array}
624	  \]
625	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
626	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
627	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
628	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
629	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
630	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
631	  \(\widetilde M\).
632	
633	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
634	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
635	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
636	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
637	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
638	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
639	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
640	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
641	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
642	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
643	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
644	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
645	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
646	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
647	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
648	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
649	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
650	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
651	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
652	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
653	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
654	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
655	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
656	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
657	
658	  \medskip
659	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
660	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
661	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
662	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
663	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
664	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
665	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
666	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
667	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
668	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
669	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
670	  naturality square for the inclusion
671	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
672	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
673	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
674	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
675	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
676	
677	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
678	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
679	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
680	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
681	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
682	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
683	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
684	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
685	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
686	
687	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
688	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
689	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
690	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
691	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
692	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
693	  % bump.
694	  \medskip
695	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
696	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
697	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
698	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
699	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
700	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
701	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
702	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
703	  needed for the affine lane.
704	\end{proof}
705	
706	\begin{remark}
707	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
708	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
709	  \begin{enumerate}
710	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
711	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
712	      \(R\)-module of sections of
713	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
714	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
715	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
716	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
717	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
718	      closed under isomorphism; hence
719	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
720	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
721	      needed for the affine lane.
722	  \end{enumerate}
723	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
724	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
725	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
726	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
727	\end{remark}
728	
729	\subsection{The pullback companion}
730	
731	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
732	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
733	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
734	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
735	
736	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints from the
737	per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison of
738	Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, packaged as a natural isomorphism of
739	right-adjoint functors (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}).
740	
741	\begin{lemma}
742	
743	\leanok
744	[Naturality packaging of the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison]
745	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardNatIso}
747	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
748	  The per-object comparison of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} assembles into a
749	  natural isomorphism of the two right-adjoint functors
750	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)}\) and
751	  \(\widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) on modules, exhibiting the
752	  pushforward dictionary as a natural transformation rather than merely a family of
753	  object isomorphisms. This is the input to the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints argument
754	  that produces the pullback companion Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}.
755	\end{lemma}
756	\begin{proof}
757	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
758	  \leanok
759	  The components are the object isomorphisms of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso};
760	  naturality is the corresponding naturality square, which holds because every
761	  constituent of the comparison is identity-on-elements.
762	\end{proof}
763	
764	\begin{lemma}
765	
766	\leanok
767	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
768	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
769	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
770	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
771	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
772	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
773	  % L1241--1256).
774	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
775	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
776	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
777	  % be affine schemes.
778	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
779	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
780	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
781	  % \begin{enumerate}
782	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
783	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
784	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
785	  % in the $S$-module $N$.
786	  % \end{enumerate}"
787	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
788	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
789	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
790	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
791	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
792	  \[
793	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
794	    \;\cong\;
795	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
796	  \]
797	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
798	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
799	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
800	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
801	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
802	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
803	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
804	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
805	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)
806	  of the same Stacks lemma of which the pushforward formula is part~(2).
807	\end{lemma}
808	
809	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
810	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
811	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."
812	\begin{proof}
813	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
814	  \leanok
815	  Pullback along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, on quasi-coherent sheaves, the
816	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
817	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
818	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
819	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
820	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
821	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
822	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
823	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
824	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
825	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
826	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
827	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong
828	  \widetilde{(R' \otimes_R M)}\). Naturality in \(M\) is the naturality of that
829	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on
830	  \(D(\varphi a)\) the localization \((R' \otimes_R M)[1/a]\) is
831	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
832	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)
833	\end{proof}
834	
835	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
836	
837	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
838	reduces, via the locality criterion
839	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
840	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
841	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
842	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
843	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
844	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
845	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
846	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
847	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
848	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
849	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
850	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
851	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
852	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
853	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
854	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
855	
856	\begin{lemma}
857	
858	\leanok
859	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
860	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
861	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
862	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
863	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
864	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
865	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
866	  % L920--926).
867	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
868	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
869	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
870	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
871	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
872	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
873	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
874	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
875	  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
876	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
877	  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
878	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
879	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
880	  by the affine-open locality criterion
881	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
882	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
883	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
884	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
885	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
886	
887	  \medskip
888	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} With the ambient data
889	  \(f : X \to S\), \(g : S' \to S\), \(f' : X' \to S'\), \(g' : X' \to X\) and a
890	  proof \(h\) that the square is cartesian (\(h : \mathrm{IsPullback}\ g'\ f'\ f\ g\),
891	  whose commutativity \(h.w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) feeds the base-change map),
892	  under the hypotheses \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) and
893	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\), the lemma is the locality reduction
894	  \[
895	    \bigl(\forall\, U : S'.\mathrm{affineOpens},\
896	      \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
897	        \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{app}\ U\bigr)\bigr)
898	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
899	    \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
900	      \mathcal{F}\bigr),
901	  \]
902	  i.e.\ it discharges the affine-open locality criterion
903	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} for the base-change map, with the
904	  per-affine-open hypothesis being exactly the affine--affine section assertion that
905	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} supplies. The content
906	  beyond the bare locality criterion is the identification of the restriction of
907	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')
908	  \subseteq S'\) with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over a
909	  chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), over which
910	  \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
911	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) makes \(\mathcal{F}\) restrict to
912	  \(\widetilde{M}\).
913	\end{lemma}
914	
915	\begin{proof}
916	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
917	  \leanok
918	  By the affine-open locality criterion
919	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the morphism
920	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} is an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
921	  as soon as its restriction to the sections over each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\)
922	  is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
923	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
924	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
925	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
926	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
927	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
928	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
929	
930	  \medskip
931	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
932	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
933	  is the image, under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
934	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
935	  \[
936	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
937	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
938	  \]
939	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
940	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
941	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
942	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
943	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
944	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
945	
946	  \medskip
947	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
948	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
949	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
950	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
951	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
952	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
953	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
954	  \begin{itemize}
955	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
956	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
957	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
958	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
959	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
960	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
961	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
962	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
963	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
964	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
965	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
966	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
967	  \end{itemize}
968	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
969	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
970	  restricted counterpart — shows that
971	  \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\), the restriction of the
972	  base-change map to sections over \(U\), is itself \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap},
973	  now formed from the cartesian square restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
974	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
975	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
976	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
977	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
978	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
979	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
980	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
981	  obligation beyond them.
982	
983	  \medskip
984	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
985	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
986	  that \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\) is an isomorphism
987	  for every affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2,
988	  precisely the affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with
989	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, by the section-level computation

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).

367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection

385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.

903	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
904	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
905	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
906	
907	  This is the genuine Mathlib-absent residue of generic freeness: the bottom of the
908	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.
909	\end{lemma}
910	
911	\begin{proof}
912	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
913	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
914	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
915	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
916	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
917	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
918	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
919	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
920	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
921	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
922	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
923	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
924	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
925	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
926	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
927	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
928	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
929	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
930	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
931	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
932	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
933	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
934	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
935	  corresponding polynomial ring.
936	
937	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
938	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
939	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
940	
941	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
942	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
943	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
944	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
945	  \begin{enumerate}
946	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
947	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
948	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
949	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
950	      \(P_d\)-modules
951	      \[
952	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
953	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
954	      \]
955	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
956	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
957	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
958	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
959	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
960	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
961	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
962	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
963	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
964	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
965	      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
966	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
967	      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
968	      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
969	      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
970	      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
971	      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
972	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
973	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
974	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
975	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
976	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
977	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
978	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
979	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
980	      completes the induction.
981	  \end{enumerate}
982	\end{proof}

984	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
985	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
986	
987	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
988	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
989	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
990	
991	\begin{lemma}
992	
993	
994	\leanok
995	[Flat localization away, finite module]
996	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
997	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
998	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
999	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1000	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
1001	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
1002	\end{lemma}
1003	\begin{proof}
1004	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1005	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
1006	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
1007	\end{proof}
1008	
1009	\begin{lemma}
1010	
1011	
1012	\leanok
1013	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1014	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1016	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1017	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra
1018	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite A B]}), and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module
1019	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite B M]}) with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the
1020	  scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such
1021	  that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is free over
1022	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
1023	\end{lemma}
1024	\begin{proof}
1025	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1026	  The \(A\)-finiteness of \(M\) is \emph{derived}, not assumed: since \(B\) is
1027	  module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\), composition along
1028	  the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\) makes \(M\) module-finite over \(A\) (transitivity
1029	  of module-finiteness, \texttt{Module.Finite.trans}). With \(M\) now a finite
1030	  \(A\)-module, \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} produces the required \(f \ne 0\) with
1031	  \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
1032	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}. The lemma is therefore strictly more general than a
1033	  repackaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: the finite \(A\)-algebra \(B\) and the
1034	  finite \(B\)-module \(M\) are the genuine inputs, and \(A\)-finiteness is a
1035	  consequence of the hypotheses rather than one of them.
1036	\end{proof}

1043	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1044	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1045	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
