1070	
1071	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1072	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1073	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1074	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1075	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1076	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1077	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1078	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1079	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1080	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1081	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1082	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1083	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1084	
1085	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
1086	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
1087	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
1088	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
1089	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
1090	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
1091	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
1092	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
1093	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
1094	\end{theorem}
1095	
1096	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1097	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1098	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1099	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1100	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1101	% by restricting to a non-empty
1102	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1103	\begin{proof}
1104	  
1105	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1106	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
1107	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
1108	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1109	
1110	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1111	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1112	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1113	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1114	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1117	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
1118	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
1119	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
1120	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
1121	  to the algebraic form.
1122	
1123	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1124	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1125	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1126	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1127	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
1128	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
1129	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
1130	
1131	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
1132	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
1133	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
1134	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
1135	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
1136	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
1137	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1138	\end{proof}

1140	\section{Mathlib status}
1141	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1142	
1143	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1144	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1145	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1146	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1147	generic-flatness argument consumes
1148	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1149	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1150	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1151	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1152	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1153	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1154	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1155	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1156	are introduced in
1157	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
1158	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
1159	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.

987	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
988	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
989	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
990	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
991	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
992	  \[
993	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
994	    \qquad
995	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
996	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
997	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
998	  \]
999	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
1000	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
1001	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
1002	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
1003	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
1004	\end{definition}

1011	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)
1012	  a presheaf of sets. The predicate \(F.\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\) asserts that
1013	  \(F\) is representable, i.e.\ that there exists an object \(Y\) of
1014	  \(\mathcal{C}\) together with a natural bijection
1015	  \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\) (the Yoneda bijection).
1016	\end{lemma}

1072	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
1073	  Explained Ch.~5).}
1074	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
1075	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
1076	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
1077	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
1078	  equipped with a tautological quotient
1079	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
1080	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
1081	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
1082	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
1083	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
1084	
1085	\end{theorem}

1087	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
1088	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1089	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
1090	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
1091	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
1092	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
1093	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
1094	\begin{proof}
1095	  
1096	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
1097	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
1098	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1099	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1100	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1101	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1102	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1103	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1104	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1105	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1106	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1107	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1108	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1109	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1110	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1111	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1112	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1113	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1114	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1115	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1116	
1117	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1118	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1119	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1120	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1121	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1122	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1123	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
1124	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
1125	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
1126	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
1127	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
1128	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
1129	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
1130	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
1131	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
1132	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
1133	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
1134	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
1135	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
1136	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
1137	\end{proof}

1008	base-change comparison, an isomorphism carrying no flatness hypothesis. The computation

1245	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1246	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is

1257	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using

1318	\begin{proof}
1319	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1320	  \leanok
1321	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1322	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1323	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1324	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence
1325	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
1326	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). Crucially, that helper bundles
1327	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1328	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1329	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1330	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1331	  the exact \(\operatorname{extendScalars}/\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) packaging that
1332	  the codomain and domain reads produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1333	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1334	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1335	
1336	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1337	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1338	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1339	  (\(\operatorname{Algebra.TensorProduct.leftAlgebra}\), multiplication in the left
1340	  factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the dictionaries,
1341	  \((\operatorname{extendScalars}\iota)\,M\) with \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the
1342	  left inclusion, tensors over the \(\operatorname{restr}_\iota\) \(A\)-action arising
1343	  from \(\iota.\mathrm{toAlgebra}\). These two \(A\)-module structures on
1344	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1345	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1346	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1347	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1348	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1349	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1350	  \[
1351	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1352	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1353	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1354	  \]
1355	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1356	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1357	
1358	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1359	  promoting along \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) yields the desired bundled
1360	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1361	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1362	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1363	\end{proof}

1406	  Let \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) be the canonical inclusion
1407	  (\(a \mapsto a \otimes 1\)). The unit of the
1408	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\)-adjunction
1409	  evaluated at the affine sheaf \(\widetilde M\), read on the global sections over
1410	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) through the tilde dictionaries
1411	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and
1412	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) unit \(\widetilde{(-)}\Gamma\)-iso, equals the algebraic unit of the
1413	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\iota_A\), namely
1414	  \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
1415	  (Mathlib's \texttt{ModuleCat.extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\ \(\iota_A\) unit).

1417	\begin{proof}
1418	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1419	  This is the affine, square-free heart of the computation: there is no pullback square and
1420	  no base-change map yet, only the comparison of two units of two adjunctions related by the
1421	  two tilde dictionaries. The geometric adjunction
1422	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^* \dashv (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\) on
1423	  sheaves of modules transports, under the tilde dictionaries which identify
1424	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*\) of a tilde with extension of scalars
1425	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\) of a tilde
1426	  with restriction of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}), to the algebraic
1427	  extension/restriction adjunction \((\iota_A^{\,*} \dashv \iota_{A\,*})\) on
1428	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these
1429	  adjunctions, naturality identifies the two units; evaluating at \(M\) gives the stated
1430	  value \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1431	\end{proof}

1995	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1996	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1997	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1998	  \mathlibok
1999	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2000	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2001	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2002	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2003	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2004	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2005	  \[
2006	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2007	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2008	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2009	  \]
2010	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2011	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2012	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2013	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2014	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2015	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2016	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2017	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2018	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2019	\end{lemma}
